claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 12 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 12 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by learning about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility rules depend on age:
- **Under 16**: Generally eligible (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: May be eligible if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me:
1. How many children do you want to check eligibility for?
2. What is each child's age (and their name, so I can keep track)?

This will help me guide you through the right questions for your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 12 years old. We live together here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me work through this with you.

So you have **Alex, who is 12 years old**. Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start. Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 12, I imagine Alex is not working full-time, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 12, so they're just at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's what I expected! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

By "in their own right," I mean benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed by Alex themselves (not benefits you receive for Alex). 

For a 12-year-old at school, this would be very unusual. Can you confirm that Alex is not claiming any benefits in their own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular 12-year-old at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is 12 and at regular school, I assume they're not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm. Apprenticeships typically start at age 16 or older.

Can you confirm Alex is not in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're way too young for that! They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes sense! Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status. You mentioned you live in Manchester, which is helpful.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Since you live in Manchester, I'm guessing you normally live in the UK. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I normally live in the UK. I'm based here in Manchester with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the child** (most common - and you've already mentioned you live with Alex)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned you live together with Alex in Manchester, it sounds like you meet the first requirement. Can you confirm you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Someone else who cares for Alex

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. If someone else is already claiming, you would need them to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide who has priority.

Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or would you be the only claimant?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I would be the only one claiming.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation.

**Is Alex in any of these situations:**

1. **In hospital** (admitted as an inpatient)
2. **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)
3. **Fostered** (living with foster carers)
4. **Living normally at home with you** (which is what you've described)

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you in Manchester. Is that correct, or is Alex in any of the other situations I mentioned?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Alex is just living normally at home with me. They're not in hospital, care, or fostered or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final check:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the council is not providing financial support for Alex's living costs or care. Since Alex lives with you at home and you're responsible for them, I'm assuming the council isn't paying for Alex's upkeep. 

Can you confirm that the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. I'm the one looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Let me get the final outcome for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Based on our assessment:

✅ Alex is 12 years old (under 16)
✅ Alex is not working full-time
✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right
✅ Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
✅ You normally live in the UK (Manchester)
✅ You live with and are responsible for Alex
✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
✅ The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

---

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

---

### **Important Information:**

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: Be aware that if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay a tax charge. This doesn't affect your eligibility to claim, but you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return.

💷 **Payment Schedule**: Payments are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

---

### **How to Apply:**

You can find the application form and more information here:
**https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

If you have any other questions about applying or the process, feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old (under 16, which meets the age requirement). Alex is not working (under the 24 hours per week limit). Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right. Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (in Manchester), meeting residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility for the child. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives normally at home and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 12-year-old child Alex. The claimant is UK resident, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and there are no disqualifying factors.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 12 years old (under 16, which meets the age requirement). Alex is not working (under the 24 hours per week limit). Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right. Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (in Manchester), meeting residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility for the child. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives normally at home and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 12-year-old child Alex. The claimant is UK resident, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and there are no disqualifying factors.
